attributes.stem:
A 73-year-old man is admitted to hospital with an acute exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). He has severe COPD (GOLD stage 4) requiring home oxygen therapy, hypertension, osteoarthritis of both knees, and mild cognitive impairment. His regular medications include tiotropium, fluticasone/salmeterol inhaler, ramipril, and prednisolone 25 mg daily for his current exacerbation. He does not smoke.

Over the past two days, he reports new onset urinary incontinence. He has no dysuria, frequency, or urgency, but states he cannot make it to the toilet in time due to increased breathlessness and fatigue when walking. He requires a walking frame and becomes short of breath after walking 5 metres.

On examination his pulse rate is 92/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 93% on 2 L/min oxygen, and temperature 37.2°C. His chest has widespread wheeze and crackles. Blood tests show white cell count 12.5 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management for his incontinence?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with functional incontinence, which occurs when physical, cognitive, or environmental barriers prevent a person from reaching the toilet in time. In this case, his severe COPD exacerbation is causing increased breathlessness and fatigue, limiting his mobility and ability to reach the toilet. This may resolve entirely after his exacerbation resolves, or elements may remain in the longer term.

Bedside commode is the most appropriate initial management as it addresses the underlying cause of his incontinence by reducing the distance he needs to walk to void. This maintains his dignity and independence while avoiding the risks associated with more invasive interventions.

Indwelling urinary catheter would be inappropriate as first-line management as it carries risks of urinary tract infection, trauma, and further decreased mobility. Catheters should be reserved for specific indications such as acute urinary retention or need for strict fluid balance monitoring.

Anticholinergic medication would not be appropriate as this is not urgency incontinence or overactive bladder. The patient has normal bladder function but cannot reach the toilet in time due to physical limitations.

Adult incontinence pads may be needed as a temporary measure but should not be the primary management strategy as they do not address the underlying cause and may impact dignity and skin integrity.

Intermittent self-catheterisation would not be appropriate as the patient has normal bladder function and emptying. This technique is typically used for patients with neurogenic bladder or retention issues.

Additional management should include physiotherapy to improve mobility, optimisation of COPD treatment, and environmental modifications such as ensuring good lighting and clear pathways to the toilet.
learningPoints: Functional incontinence occurs when physical, cognitive or environmental barriers prevent a person from reaching the toilet in time.
Management of functional incontinence should focus on addressing underlying mobility and environmental factors rather than bladder function.
Urinary catheters should be avoided unless absolutely necessary due to risk of infection and decreased mobility.
references: DeMaagd GA, Davenport TC. Management of urinary incontinence. Pharmacy and Therapeutics. 2012 Jun;37(6):345. Available online at ; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3411204/

Soni N, Banerjee J, Gunasekaran V, Saini M, Singh V, Kumar P, Chakrawarty A, Chatterjee P, Dey AB. Association of geriatric syndromes in older adults with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Aging Medicine. 2022 Jun;5(2):106-12. Available online at ; https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdf/10.1002/agm2.12208
